revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Hokenoyama Kofun]][[ja:ホケノ山古墳]]


{{Short description|Ancient Japanese tomb}}
{{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]]|type = {{ill|scallop-shaped kofun|ja|帆立貝形古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/scallop-shaped kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}
| image       = File:180427 Hokenoyama Kofun zenkei.jpg
| coordinates = {{Coord|34.539861|N|135.844889|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}
| location    = {{ill|Sakurai|qid=Q127026|1=Sakurai, Nara}}
| architectural_styles = {{ill|Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun|qid=Q11608504|1=Makimuku-type keyhole-shaped kofun}}
}}

{{ill|Hokenoyama Kofun|en|Hokenoyama Kofun|qid=Q11337664|lt=Hokenoyama Kofun|1=Hokenoyama Kofun}} is a scallop shaped [[Kofun]] containing several burial chambers at the site of the [[Makimuku ruins]] in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}, Japan.<ref name="webarchiveorg-2023a">{{Cite web |date=2023-11-08 |title=Hokenoyama Kofun [ホケノ山古墳] Long Barrow : The Megalithic Portal and Megalith Map |url=https://www.megalithic.co.uk/article.php?sid=53966 |access-date=2023-12-07 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231108225956/https://www.megalithic.co.uk/article.php?sid=53966 |archive-date=2023-11-08 }}</ref><ref name="ki11er-2017a">{{Cite book |last=Ki11er |first=J. Edward |url=http://dx.doi.org/10.1515/9780824862848 |title=Himiko and Japan's Elusive Chiefdom of Yamatai |date=2017-12-18 |publisher=University of Hawaii Press |doi=10.1515/9780824862848 |isbn=978-0-8248-6284-8}}</ref>{{rp|249}}
It is dated to around 250 AD.<ref name="ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|253}}

The tomb is located to the east of the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|qid=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}}.<ref name="ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250–251}} A road cuts through its southeast extension as can be seen in the image to the right.<ref name="ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250–251}} It  is decorated with [[Fukiishi]] stones from the Makimuku river.<ref name="ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250–251}}

It has a distinctive scallop-shaped design, the Kofun measures approximately 80 meters in length. Over time, certain details of its angular contours have become less distinct due to natural wear and erosion.<ref name="webarchiveorg-2023a" />

Internally, the Kofun houses several burial pits and chambers. One of its noteworthy features is a reconstructed cist that contains a wooden coffin.[[File: Hokenoyama-kofun sekikan.jpg|thumb|sarcophagus from the kofun]] This site has garnered attention from scholars who posit that the Hokenoyama Kofun might be among the earliest examples of the Kofun tumulus, suggesting its origin may coincide with the early [[Kofun period]].<ref name="webarchiveorg-2023a" />

The 1995 excavation found a wooden coffin and a stone paved floor.t.<ref name="ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250–251}} Grave goods from the local area but also the {{ill|Tōkai region|en|Tōkai region|qid=Q398989|lt=Tōkai region|1=Tōkai region}} and [[Seto Inland Sea]]. A mirror from it is kept in {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}}.<ref name="ki11er-2017a" />{{rp|250–251}}

Access to the site, though straightforward, requires navigation through a notably narrow road. However, the nearby parking facility accommodates visitors.<ref name="webarchiveorg-2023a" />

The area surrounding the tomb is also of archaeological significance, containing other sites such as the {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|qid=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}} and the [[Makimuku Ishizuka Kofun]].<ref name="webarchiveorg-2023a" />
